title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics.

sections.body:
A new review summarizes evidence that **urolithin A** supplementation supports mitochondrial health and muscle endurance in older adults. Separately, dermatology trials continue to favor **topical retinoids** as the best-evidenced anti-aging actives, while early data on **NAD+ precursors** remains promising but not yet conclusive.

- Prioritize interventions with human RCTs over anecdote.
- Sunscreen remains the highest-ROI "anti-aging" habit.

sources.title: Urolithin A and mitochondrial function in aging

sections.body: A randomized study found that **2x weekly low-volume HIIT** (e.g. 4×4 minute intervals) improved VO2max comparably to longer moderate-intensity sessions in women, with better adherence. Researchers emphasized progressive overload and adequate recovery to avoid overtraining.

sources.title: Low-volume HIIT and cardiorespiratory fitness in women

sections.body: Updated physiotherapy guidance reaffirms **supervised pelvic floor muscle training** as first-line for stress urinary incontinence. Key point: *quality over quantity* — correct activation with a clinician beats high-rep self-directed routines.

For **HER2+ breast cancer**, conference coverage highlighted next-generation **antibody-drug conjugates** extending progression-free survival in pretreated patients. Research into **p53-pathway** targeting remains largely preclinical. Always discuss options with an oncologist — "alternative" should mean evidence-based adjuncts, not replacements for standard care.

sources.title: Antibody-drug conjugates in HER2+ breast cancer
Targeting the p53 pathway: state of the field

sections.body:
A meta-analysis links **consistent sleep/wake timing** to fewer nighttime awakenings — schedule regularity outperformed total time in bed for sleep continuity. On supplements, **magnesium glycinate** and low-dose **melatonin (0.5–1 mg)** had modest support; high-dose melatonin did not improve outcomes.

1. Fixed wake time, even on weekends.
2. Dim, cool, screen-free wind-down.
3. Re-evaluate caffeine after 2 p.m.
